items.question_text: When holding instructions include a charted pattern, what may be omitted from the holding instructions?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-1b2: When the assigned procedure or route includes a charted pattern, you may omit all holding instructions except the charted holding direction and the statement 'as published.' Always issue complete holding instructions when the pilot requests them.

choices.text: Only the leg length may be omitted.
All holding instructions except the charted holding direction and 'as published.'
Only the EFC time may be omitted.
All holding instructions may be omitted entirely.

items.question_text: When should the EFC (Expect Further Clearance) time NOT be specified in a holding clearance?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-1c: Do not specify this item if no delay is expected.

choices.text: When the aircraft is holding at a published holding pattern.
When no delay is expected.
When the holding is less than 10 minutes.
When the holding fix is the destination airport.

items.question_text: When delay is expected, how far in advance must holding clearance items be issued before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-1c4: When delay is expected, issue items in subparagraphs a and b at least 5 minutes before the aircraft is estimated to reach the clearance limit. If less than 5 minutes from the fix, issue immediately.

choices.text: At least 10 minutes before.
At least 5 minutes before.
At least 3 minutes before.
At least 15 minutes before.

items.question_text: When should the phrase 'DELAY INDEFINITE' be used?
items.explanation: Per the note in 4-6-1: 'Delay indefinite' should be used when an accurate estimate of the delay time and the reason for the delay cannot immediately be determined (e.g., disabled aircraft on runway, sector saturation, weather below minimums).

choices.text: When the delay exceeds 30 minutes.
When an accurate delay estimate and reason cannot immediately be determined.
When holding at the destination airport.
For all holding clearances at non-towered airports.

items.question_text: When additional holding is expected at more than one fix in the facility's area, how should the delay be communicated?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-1c1: When more than one fix is involved, state the total additional en route delay and omit specific fixes.

choices.text: List each fix and its individual delay.
State the total additional en route delay and omit specific fixes.
Only provide delay for the first holding fix.
Tell the pilot to expect further information at each fix.

items.question_text: If no delay is expected, when should a clearance beyond the clearance limit be issued?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-2a: As soon as possible and, whenever possible, at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.

choices.text: At least 10 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.
As soon as possible, and at least 5 minutes before the fix when possible.
Only when the aircraft requests it.
Within 3 minutes of the fix.

items.question_text: When should the phrase 'VIA LAST ROUTING CLEARED' be used in a clearance beyond a fix?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-2b2(b): Use 'via last routing cleared' only when the most recently issued routing to the new clearance limit is valid and verbiage will be reduced.

choices.text: Any time a clearance beyond a fix is issued.
Only when the most recently issued routing is valid and verbiage will be reduced.
Only for military aircraft.
When the pilot requests the previously cleared route.

items.question_text: What three items must be included when issuing clearance beyond a clearance limit?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-2b: Include (1) clearance limit or approach clearance, (2) route of flight (complete details or 'via last routing cleared'), and (3) assigned altitude if different from present altitude.

choices.text: Holding instructions, EFC, and route.
Clearance limit/approach, route of flight, and assigned altitude if different.
Destination airport, SID, and altitude.
Clearance limit, EFC, and transponder code.

items.question_text: When arrival delays reach or are anticipated to reach how many minutes, must total delay information be issued?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-3b: When arrival delays reach or are anticipated to reach 30 minutes, take the specified actions.

choices.text: 15 minutes.
30 minutes.
45 minutes.
60 minutes.

items.question_text: EN ROUTE: For 30+ minute arrival delays, who must issue the total delay information and when?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-3b1: The center responsible for transferring control must issue total delay info as soon as possible after aircraft enters the center's area. Whenever possible, by the first center controller to communicate with the aircraft.

choices.text: The destination tower when the aircraft checks in.
The first center controller to communicate with the aircraft, as soon as possible after entering the center's area.
The approach controller during initial descent.
The flight service station on initial contact.

items.question_text: When may the requirement to issue total delay information for 30+ minute delays be omitted?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-3b3: Unless a pilot requests delay information, the actions may be omitted when total delay information is available to pilots via ATIS.

choices.text: When the delay is expected to be less than 45 minutes.
When total delay information is available to pilots via ATIS.
When the aircraft is outside the terminal area.
When the pilot is on a SID/STAR.

items.question_text: When must the direction of holding pattern turns be specified in holding instructions?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-4e: Specify direction of turns only if left turns are to be made, the pilot requests it, or you consider it necessary.

choices.text: Always; direction of turns is mandatory in all holding instructions.
Only if left turns are to be made, the pilot requests it, or you consider it necessary.
Only for non-published holding patterns.
Only when holding at a NAVAID.

items.question_text: When leg length is specified in holding instructions using DME or RNAV, in what unit is it given?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-4d: Leg length in miles if DME or RNAV is to be used. Specify leg length in minutes if the pilot requests it or you consider it necessary.

choices.text: In minutes by default.
In miles if DME or RNAV; in minutes if pilot requests or you consider it necessary.
In nautical miles always.
In kilometers for international operations.

items.question_text: When must maximum holding airspeed advisories be issued?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-4f: Issue maximum holding airspeed advisories when an aircraft is: (1) approved to exceed max airspeed of a pattern and cleared into a pattern that protects for the greater speed, (2) observed deviating from the holding pattern airspace area, or (3) cleared into an airspeed restricted holding pattern where the icon has not been published.

choices.text: During all holding clearances.
When cleared into an airspeed-restricted holding pattern where the icon has not been published, or deviating, or approved to exceed max speed.
Only when the pilot requests airspeed information.
Only at high-altitude holding patterns.

items.question_text: When ceiling is less than 800 feet or visibility is less than 2 miles, what holding restriction applies near an ILS?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-8: Do not authorize aircraft to hold below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the ILS OM (or fix used in lieu) and the airport.

choices.text: No holding within 5 miles of the localizer.
Do not hold below 5,000 feet AGL inbound on/within 1 SM of localizer between OM and airport.
Do not hold below 3,000 feet AGL within the localizer critical area.
Holding is prohibited entirely when weather is below 800-2.

items.question_text: What weather conditions trigger the ILS holding restriction per 4-6-8?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-8: When the official weather observation indicates a ceiling of less than 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles.

choices.text: Ceiling less than 1,000 feet or visibility less than 3 miles.
Ceiling less than 800 feet or visibility less than 2 miles.
Ceiling less than 500 feet or visibility less than 1 mile.
Ceiling less than 200 feet or RVR less than 2,400 feet.
